summary: Raw early‑'67 set shines on Morning Dew and Viola Lee Blues
blurb: The three‑track set captures the raw energy of early ’67 with a soaring Morning Dew and blistering Viola Lee Blues. The soundboard tape is fair, marked by hiss and occasional muddiness, but its historic performance outweighs the technical flaws.
review: On January 14, 1967 at San Francisco’s Polo Field, the Grateful Dead delivered a brief yet potent three‑song set that has become a snapshot of their embryonic psychedelic era. Garcia’s guitar on “Morning Dew” soars with emotional intensity, only marred by a tiny slip near the song’s climax, while his riff‑driven assault on “Viola Lee Blues” erupts into raw, free‑form jamming that epitomizes the band’s early improvisational spirit. Phil Lesh anchors the low end with a solid bass line, occasionally nudging the band forward with a vocal cue (“slow down”). Bill Kreutzmann powers the rhythm section, and there are hints of a second drummer later in the set—likely Mickey Hart—but the timing feels loose. A lone flute (presumably Charles Lloyd) drifts into “Good Morning Little School Girl,” an out‑of‑key flourish that many find distracting. The sole soundboard source is marred by tape hiss, drop‑outs, and a muddy low end; however, fans often describe this lo‑fi character as adding nostalgic atmosphere to an otherwise historically priceless document of the Human Be‑In tour.

best_recording.reason: Only soundboard capture; despite hiss and mud it preserves the historic early‑'67 performance with clear moments of Morning Dew and Viola Lee Blues.

key_highlights: Morning Dew showcases Garcia’s soaring solo and emotional intensity.
Viola Lee Blues delivers raw, free‑form jamming that epitomizes the era.
Historical significance: one of the earliest recorded Dead sets from the Human Be‑In tour.
Recording is fair – tape hiss and occasional muddiness give a lo‑fi nostalgic feel.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s guitar tone shines, delivering an impassioned solo on Morning Dew and driving riffs on Viola Lee Blues, with only a minor slip near the end of Dew.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh lays down a solid bass foundation throughout; his mix is low but his occasional vocal cue (“slow down”) adds character.
band_performance.Pigpen: Keyboard presence is minimal; the notable flute on Good Morning Little School Girl feels out‑of‑key and distracts from Pigpen’s usual organ vibe.
band_performance.Mickey: Possible second‑drummer cameo is hinted at later in the set, though timing feels loose and opinions vary.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann delivers powerful, propulsive drumming that drives the jam sections.
